name: fancy arbalest
shortDescription: A fancy arbalest with polished fittings, carved stock, and the heavy patience of a siege weapon dressed for court display. It draws attention from fortress officers and collectors alike, especially when a buyer wants precision machinery that also looks expensive on a rack.
flavorText: “A refined arbalest still waits with the manners of a locked gate.”

name: arbalest with gold
shortDescription: An arbalest with gold worked into its frame, turning a practical missile engine into a costly declaration of rank. Captains may grumble at the ornament, but noble guards and private armories pay well for hardware that looks like authority before it is cranked.
flavorText: “Gold does not speed the bolt, but it does slow the bargaining.”

name: hand crossbow
shortDescription: A hand crossbow compact enough for saddle bags, alley work, and merchants who prefer discreet insurance. It lacks the reach of larger bows, but its small frame gives it a ready market among scouts, guards, and anyone traveling through cities after dark.
flavorText: “Small enough to hide, serious enough to respect.”

name: fabled elaborate hand crossbow
shortDescription: A fabled elaborate hand crossbow covered in delicate mechanisms, silver pins, and charmwork too fine for careless fingers. Collectors prize it as engineering art, while practical buyers whisper that its most dangerous feature is making confidence feel pocket-sized.
flavorText: “A tiny machine with a legend larger than most siege engines.”

name: hellfire crossbow
shortDescription: A hellfire crossbow darkened by heat marks and red enchantment channels, sold only to buyers willing to sign for careful storage. Its reputation brings demon hunters, war mages, and reckless nobles to the counter, all pretending caution was their first plan.
flavorText: “Some crossbows threaten. This one looks like it remembers the furnace.”

name: simple crossbow
shortDescription: A simple crossbow of plain stock, serviceable bow, and no heroic pretensions. It sells steadily because militias, caravans, and hunters need dependable tools more often than legends, and because repairs can be handled by people who own ordinary glue.
flavorText: “Plain gear keeps more roads open than heroic gear.”

name: fabled sniper crossbow
shortDescription: A fabled sniper crossbow built around long patience, silent fittings, and a draw that rewards cold discipline. Border towers, royal scouts, and collectors of unnervingly precise instruments compete for it, though merchants know the name alone makes buyers lower their voices.
flavorText: “Its bolts travel farther than most rumors, and with better aim.”

name: strong crossbow
shortDescription: A strong crossbow with reinforced limbs and sturdy hardware, made for repeated heavy pulls without complaint. It is not especially graceful, but buyers trust it for walls, wagons, and watch posts where reliability matters more than pretty engraving.
flavorText: “A strong crossbow is a promise made in wood, cord, and stubborn metal.”

name: fabled blade of embers
shortDescription: The fabled blade of embers carries a low inner glow, as if a forge coal never quite went out inside the steel. Duelists, fire cults, and collectors seek it for very different reasons, but every careful trader stores it away from wax, oil, and loose cloth.
flavorText: “A blade that remembers heat is never fully at rest.”

name: dagger
shortDescription: A dagger of ordinary size and unmistakable usefulness, equally at home in a belt, boot, toolkit, or locked evidence chest. Its market is broad because small blades solve small problems quickly, whether the buyer admits the problem is rope, dinner, or danger.
flavorText: “The common dagger has more biographies than most kings.”

name: scarce starcourt dagger
shortDescription: A scarce starcourt dagger with pale celestial inlay and a narrow courtly profile, prized by nobles who enjoy quiet symbols of rank. It is small enough for ceremony, sharp enough for seriousness, and rare enough that every sale produces three new stories.
flavorText: “Starcourt steel always seems to arrive under excellent lighting.”

name: scarce starcourt short sword
shortDescription: A scarce starcourt short sword bearing bright sky-metal accents and the polished balance of a dueling court. It appeals to officers, collectors, and ceremonial guards who want something shorter than a battlefield blade but grander than a side knife.
flavorText: “Shorter blades can still cast long shadows at court.”

name: fancy short sword
shortDescription: A fancy short sword with decorated grip, bright guard, and a blade kept polished for both use and admiration. It sells well to traveling nobles, guild champions, and anyone who believes practical steel should not be forced to look poor.
flavorText: “A short sword becomes fancy when even the scabbard expects compliments.”

name: gold infused dagger
shortDescription: A gold infused dagger worked with warm metallic veins that catch candlelight along the blade. Most buyers claim to want it for enchantment, status, or collection value, but merchants know the shine does half the selling before the edge is discussed.
flavorText: “Gold in a dagger is rarely subtle, but neither is pride.”

name: fabled dagger of betrayal
shortDescription: The fabled dagger of betrayal is infamous for changing hands in stories that end badly for someone. Spymasters, curse scholars, and collectors of unpleasant history pay dangerous sums for it, while honest traders keep the receipt language unusually precise.
flavorText: “Some names are warnings that survived the owner.”

name: scarce blade of accuracy
shortDescription: A scarce blade of accuracy balanced with uncanny care, its point seeming to find the line a hand intended before the hand fully knows it. Duelists, surgeons of questionable legality, and precision-obsessed collectors all value its neat, confident motion.
flavorText: “Accuracy makes a small blade feel much larger.”

name: ironclan dagger
shortDescription: An ironclan dagger forged in the compact, hard-edged style of mountain halls, practical enough for work and severe enough for ceremony. It lacks courtly softness, which is exactly why clan buyers, guards, and stern collectors keep asking after it.
flavorText: “Ironclan daggers do not flatter the palm; they test it.”

name: ironclan short sword
shortDescription: An ironclan short sword with a heavy guard, plain finish, and dependable mountain-forged spine. It is built for durability over flourish, a fact that makes it popular with soldiers who would rather trust a blade than admire one.
flavorText: “The clans polish reputation by refusing to polish too much else.”

name: tiny dagger
shortDescription: A tiny dagger made for sleeves, small tasks, and buyers who understand that size and danger do not always argue in the same direction. Jewelers, cooks, gamblers, and suspicious couriers all find uses for it, though few agree on which use is respectable.
flavorText: “A tiny dagger fits neatly into excuses.”

name: quickblade
shortDescription: A quickblade is a light, fast knife balanced for sudden movement and nimble handling. Street duelists, scouts, and stage fighters love the name, while merchants love how quickly buyers test its flick and start imagining themselves faster than they are.
flavorText: “The quickblade sells speed first and steel second.”

name: short sword
shortDescription: A short sword of straightforward campaign design, easier to carry than a full blade and more serious than a long knife. It is dependable stock for guards, sailors, caravan hands, and anyone who needs steel that does not demand a wide room.
flavorText: “The short sword survives because cramped places still have arguments.”

name: slashing dagger
shortDescription: A slashing dagger with a curved, aggressive edge built for quick cuts rather than polite utility. Its buyers are usually fighters, collectors, or theatrical suppliers, and every careful shop keeps it sheathed before curious fingers make the demonstration expensive.
flavorText: “A slashing dagger turns motion into punctuation.”

name: small blade
shortDescription: A small blade of plain make, useful for trimming cord, cleaning game, opening parcels, and reminding buyers that simple steel is never truly harmless. It is common inventory, but common inventory often leaves the shelf fastest.
flavorText: “Small blades do not need legends to earn counter space.”

name: rough spiked flail
shortDescription: A rough spiked flail with crude chain, battered head, and a look that suggests function arrived long before refinement. Militias, arena handlers, and grim collectors buy it for raw intimidation, though merchants hang it where it cannot swing into better merchandise.
flavorText: “A rough flail makes every nearby object nervous.”

name: decorative green flail
shortDescription: A decorative green flail painted and trimmed for spectacle, festival guards, or noble houses with unusual colors. Despite the ornament, its chain and head remain quite real, giving it the profitable confusion of pageantry that can still bruise a door.
flavorText: “Green paint does not make a flail friendly.”

name: fabled flail of burning death
shortDescription: The fabled flail of burning death carries a furious heat in its head and a name that refuses understatement. War collectors, fire priests, and theatrical tyrants compete for it, while cautious traders write storage warnings in larger ink than the price.
flavorText: “Subtlety was not invited to this enchantment.”

name: fabled flail of frozen death
shortDescription: The fabled flail of frozen death is rimed with cold charmwork, its metal seeming to remember winter even beside a hearth. Frost cults, northern champions, and curio vaults value it highly, though packers complain that it makes nearby rope unpleasant to handle.
flavorText: “Some relics make the room colder before anyone asks why.”

name: gold spiked flail
shortDescription: A gold spiked flail fitted with bright metal over a brutally practical shape, created for buyers who want wealth and threat in one swinging object. Its shine draws nobles; its spikes remind everyone else that ornament did not replace purpose.
flavorText: “Gold makes many things prettier. This remains a flail.”

name: large flail
shortDescription: A large flail with a heavy head and enough reach to make handlers step back during inspection. It is awkward in crowded shops but popular with fortress buyers, arena suppliers, and collectors who decorate halls with objects guests instinctively avoid.
flavorText: “A large flail asks for space and usually gets it.”

name: large gold flail
shortDescription: A large gold flail built for spectacle, intimidation, and expensive bad decisions. The gilding gives it noble appeal, while the size keeps it firmly in the realm of serious weaponry; merchants price both the metal and the silence it creates.
flavorText: “A gold flail is what happens when fear gets a patron.”

name: long flail
shortDescription: A long flail with extended handle and chain, made for reach, leverage, and handlers who understand the cost of careless swings. It sells to trained guards, battlefield veterans, and anyone who has enough room to practice without losing lamps.
flavorText: “The long flail punishes enthusiasm before it punishes enemies.”

name: long gold flail
shortDescription: A long gold flail with ornamental fittings stretched along a weapon already difficult to ignore. It is favored by wealthy champions, ceremonial guards, and collectors who believe a display wall should look both royal and faintly hazardous.
flavorText: “Length, gold, and chains: the three languages of excess.”

name: pitch black flail
shortDescription: A pitch black flail coated in dark finish that swallows torchlight around its head and chain. Night guards, grim orders, and collectors of intimidating equipment seek it, while merchants find it oddly hard to dust because nobody wants to touch it twice.
flavorText: “Black iron makes silence look heavier.”

name: royal blue flail
shortDescription: A royal blue flail trimmed with bright enamel and courtly color, balancing noble spectacle with blunt reality. Palace guards, parade companies, and collectors of heraldic weapons prize it because it can match a banner while still discouraging approach.
flavorText: “Royal blue is not a warning color, but it tries.”

name: royal long flail
shortDescription: A royal long flail made for ceremonial reach and dramatic command presence, with a polished haft and fittings fit for palace walls. It is more difficult to carry than a title, but buyers with enough attendants rarely worry about such details.
flavorText: “Royal weapons often assume someone else is clearing the room.”

name: morningstar of planets
shortDescription: The morningstar of planets is a cosmic-themed mace whose head bears orbit-like bands and starry metalwork. Scholars argue over whether the pattern is symbolic or enchanted; merchants simply note that celestial weapons attract wealthy buyers with excellent posture.
flavorText: “Nothing says astronomy like a spiked argument.”

name: golden eveningstar
shortDescription: A golden eveningstar with radiant fittings and a heavy spiked head, elegant enough for ceremony and direct enough for any guard captain. It sells best to houses that enjoy twilight symbolism, gilded halls, and making guests count the exits.
flavorText: “The eveningstar shines brightest when negotiations dim.”

name: eveningstar mace
shortDescription: An eveningstar mace of classic spiked design, weighty in the hand and easy to understand across any language barrier. It is practical stock for guards, militias, and collectors who prefer weapons with simple silhouettes and very clear intentions.
flavorText: “Some maces explain themselves at a glance.”

name: scarce eveningstar of damage
shortDescription: A scarce eveningstar of damage etched with runes that promise harder impact and fewer second chances. Its name lacks poetry but not market appeal, drawing arena fighters, quartermasters, and charm-hunters who trust blunt labels more than elegant myths.
flavorText: “A plain enchantment name can still ruin fine armor.”

name: fancy eveningstar mace
shortDescription: A fancy eveningstar mace with polished spikes, decorated haft, and enough shine to turn a brutal design into noble display. It is popular with ceremonial guards and collectors who insist their taste is refined, despite the object being mostly points and weight.
flavorText: “Refinement is sometimes just spikes arranged symmetrically.”

name: gold morningstar mace
shortDescription: A gold morningstar mace whose gleaming head makes a severe weapon look like royal inventory. It is rarely purchased by humble buyers, but courts, rich guards, and trophy rooms understand the value of a mace that photographs well by torchlight.
flavorText: “Gold does not soften the blow, only the accounting.”

name: large eveningstar mace
shortDescription: A large eveningstar mace with extra reach, a heavy head, and the sort of presence that makes shop assistants clear a wider path. Its buyers value size as much as craftsmanship, especially when a weapon must impress before it is ever lifted.
flavorText: “Large maces begin negotiations from across the room.”

name: large gold eveningstar mace
shortDescription: A large gold eveningstar mace built for opulent intimidation, carrying enough metal and ornament to satisfy both treasurers and tyrants. It is costly to buy, costly to ship, and difficult to display without making every other weapon look underdressed.
flavorText: “A golden threat is still a threat, only better lit.”

name: large gold morningstar mace
shortDescription: A large gold morningstar mace with a radiant spiked head and a haft meant for ceremonial strength. Noble armories treat it as a centerpiece, while practical captains quietly ask whether the cheaper plain version hits just as hard.
flavorText: “The answer is yes, but nobody pays extra for that answer.”

name: large morningstar mace
shortDescription: A large morningstar mace of heavy service make, designed for buyers who measure confidence by weight. Fortress guards, militias, and rough collectors value it for the honest simplicity of a spiked head attached to a serious handle.
flavorText: “A large morningstar has never needed clever marketing.”

name: long gold mace
shortDescription: A long gold mace with extended reach and bright noble fittings, more staff-like than compact and much harder to ignore. It moves well in ceremonies, less well in cramped wagons, and perfectly in the imagination of buyers who like being seen from a distance.
flavorText: “A long gold mace is half weapon, half announcement.”

name: scarce mace of eos
shortDescription: A scarce mace of eos linked to dawn-bright legends, with pale metalwork that catches early light beautifully. Temple guards, eastern collectors, and sunrise orders prize it, though merchants mostly appreciate that anything named for dawn sells well before noon.
flavorText: “Dawn is free. This mace is not.”

name: scarce mace of far power
shortDescription: A scarce mace of far power carries enchantment marks associated with distant force, old battlemages, and stories that stretch beyond the wielder’s arm. Buyers ask complicated questions about range; merchants ask simpler ones about payment and safe packing.
flavorText: “Power is always more impressive when it claims to travel.”

name: fabled mace of high power
shortDescription: The fabled mace of high power is as direct as legendary naming gets: heavy, enchanted, and expensive enough to make accountants pale. It attracts champions, war colleges, and collectors who prefer obvious strength to subtle mystery.
flavorText: “Some legends whisper. This one invoices loudly.”

name: morningstar mace
shortDescription: A morningstar mace with a spiked head and dependable battlefield shape, common enough to be understood but serious enough to deserve respect. It fills guard racks, militia stores, and weapon stalls where buyers want no confusion about what they are purchasing.
flavorText: “The morningstar mace is not graceful, but grace rarely dents helmets.”
